walkthrough.title: Choose Your Investigators
walkthrough.text: Each of us selects one investigator card, which shows our character's name, health, sanity, and unique ability. For our first game, let's use Roland Banks and Wendy Adams, two investigators with complementary strengths.

walkthrough.title: Gather Investigator Decks
walkthrough.text: Let's grab the pre-built starter deck for each investigator. Each deck has exactly thirty cards, including assets, events, and skill cards tailored to that investigator's strengths. Roland's deck leans into combat and clue-finding, while Wendy's focuses on evasion and luck manipulation.

walkthrough.title: Set Out Investigator Cards and Mini-Cards
walkthrough.text: Let's place each investigator card face up in front of us. Next to each investigator, place their mini-card, which is used as a map marker to show our location. We each also take a resource token and a card to form our starting hand.

walkthrough.title: Take Starting Resources and Draw Opening Hands
walkthrough.text: Let's each take five resource tokens from the token pool and place them near our investigator card. Then we each draw five cards from our investigator deck to form our opening hand. Resources are our currency for playing cards during the game.

walkthrough.title: Perform Mulligan
walkthrough.text: We may set aside any number of cards from our hand, draw that many replacements, then shuffle the set-aside cards back into our deck. This is our one chance to improve our starting hand before the scenario begins.

walkthrough.title: Select the Scenario
walkthrough.text: For a first game, we will use "The Gathering," the first scenario in the Night of the Zealot campaign. Find the scenario reference card, which lists all the special setup instructions we need to follow.

walkthrough.title: Build the Agenda Deck
walkthrough.text: The agenda deck represents the evil forces working against us, and it advances when doom tokens accumulate to the threshold printed on the current agenda card. This is our countdown clock.

walkthrough.title: Build the Act Deck
walkthrough.text: The act deck represents our progress through the scenario. We advance acts by spending the required number of clue tokens, so collecting clues is how we move the story forward and ultimately win.

walkthrough.title: Prepare the Chaos Bag
walkthrough.text: The bag contains numbered tokens, an elder sign token, an auto-fail token, and several special symbol tokens. We will draw from this bag every time we attempt a skill test.

walkthrough.title: Set Up Locations
walkthrough.text: Let's lay out the location cards specified by the scenario setup instructions. Place them unrevealed side up unless the instructions say otherwise. Each location shows a shroud value on its revealed side, which tells us how hard it is to investigate there. Connect adjacent locations as shown in the scenario layout.

walkthrough.title: Place Investigators at Starting Location
walkthrough.text: Both investigators begin at the same location in The Gathering. Reveal that location by flipping it to its other side, which shows its shroud value and any clues we place on it.

walkthrough.title: Place Clues on Revealed Locations
walkthrough.text: Let's place clue tokens on our starting location. The number of clues equals the value printed on the location, sometimes modified by the number of investigators. These clues are what we need to collect through investigate actions to advance the act deck and push the story forward.

walkthrough.title: Build the Encounter Deck
walkthrough.text: This deck contains treacheries and enemies that we will draw during the mythos phase. Place the encounter deck face down where both investigators can reach it.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Token Pool
walkthrough.text: Let's organize the token supply within easy reach. We need resource tokens, clue tokens, doom tokens, damage tokens, and horror tokens. Keeping these sorted and accessible will save us time during play, since we will be adding and removing tokens constantly throughout the scenario.

walkthrough.title: Follow Remaining Scenario Instructions
walkthrough.text: Some scenarios place specific enemies, add extra encounter sets, or begin with a story passage. Complete every step listed before we begin round one.

walkthrough.title: Ready to Play
walkthrough.text: Let's make sure everything is in place. Each investigator should have their card, five resources, five cards in hand, and their mini-card on the starting location. The agenda and act decks are assembled, the chaos bag is filled, and the encounter deck is shuffled. We are ready to begin the investigation.

summary.title: Ready to Play
bullets: Each investigator: card + 5 resources + 5-card hand + mini-card on location
Agenda deck, act deck, chaos bag, encounter deck all ready

walkthrough.title: Round Structure Overview
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how a round flows. Every round has four phases in this exact order: Mythos, Investigation, Enemy, and Upkeep. We skip the Mythos phase on round one since the scenario just started, but every round after that begins with Mythos. This cycle repeats until the scenario ends.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase - Add Doom
walkthrough.text: First, we place one doom token on the current agenda card. Doom is the enemy's progress counter, and when the total doom in play reaches the threshold printed on the agenda, the agenda advances. Agenda advances are almost always very bad for us.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase - Check Doom Threshold
walkthrough.text: Count all doom tokens in play, including doom on the agenda card and doom on any enemies or locations that have accumulated it. If the total meets or exceeds the agenda's threshold number, the agenda advances and all doom is removed from play.

walkthrough.title: Mythos Phase - Draw Encounter Cards
walkthrough.text: These cards are either treacheries that take effect immediately or enemies that spawn and engage us. This is how the game throws new obstacles at us every single round, so we must always be ready for surprises.

walkthrough.title: Investigation Phase - Three Actions Each
walkthrough.text: Each investigator gets three actions per round, taken one at a time in player order. We can spend these actions in any combination we choose, and we can even take the same action multiple times. These three actions are the heartbeat of the game.

walkthrough.title: Action - Draw a Card
walkthrough.text: Let's learn our first action type. We can spend one action to draw one card from our investigator deck. Cards are our tools, weapons, allies, and tricks, so keeping our hand stocked is important. Drawing costs a precious action though, so we should weigh it against other needs.

walkthrough.title: Action - Gain a Resource
walkthrough.text: Resources pay for the cards in our hand, and many powerful assets cost three or more resources. Planning our economy early means we can play key cards when we need them most.

walkthrough.title: Action - Play a Card
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how to play cards from our hand. We spend one action and pay the card's resource cost to put an asset or event into play. Assets stay on the table and provide ongoing benefits like weapons, tools, or allies. Events resolve their effect immediately and then go to the discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Action - Move
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about movement. We spend one action to move our investigator from our current location to one connected location. When we enter a new location that has not been revealed yet, we flip it over and place clues on it equal to its clue value. Movement is how we explore the scenario map and find new clues to collect.

walkthrough.title: Action - Investigate
walkthrough.text: We spend one action and make an intellect skill test against the location's shroud value. If we succeed, we pick up one clue token from that location and add it to our investigator card. Clues are our primary path to victory since they advance the act deck.

walkthrough.title: Action - Fight
walkthrough.text: If we succeed, we deal one damage to the enemy, plus any bonus damage from our weapon. When an enemy takes damage equal to its health, it is defeated and placed in the encounter discard pile.

walkthrough.title: Action - Evade
walkthrough.text: Let's learn evasion as an alternative to fighting. We spend one action and make an agility skill test against the enemy's evade value. If we succeed, the enemy is exhausted and we are no longer engaged with it. Evading is great when we cannot defeat an enemy or when we need to slip away to investigate a different location.

walkthrough.title: Action - Engage and Activate
walkthrough.text: Let's cover two more action types. We can spend one action to engage an enemy at our location, pulling it off another investigator and onto ourselves. We can also spend one action to activate an ability on a card in play that has an action trigger arrow printed on it. Both of these are situational but important when the moment calls for them.

walkthrough.title: Skill Tests - How They Work
walkthrough.text: Let's break down skill tests step by step. First, determine which skill we are testing and what the difficulty number is. Then we may commit cards from our hand that have matching skill icons to boost our value. Next, we draw one chaos token from the bag and apply its modifier. If our total meets or exceeds the difficulty, we succeed.

walkthrough.title: Skill Tests - Committing Cards
walkthrough.text: Each matching icon adds one to our skill value for that test. Committed cards are discarded after the test resolves, whether we succeed or fail.

walkthrough.title: Chaos Tokens - Numbers and Special Symbols
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the chaos bag contents. Number tokens apply their value as a modifier, usually negative. The Elder Sign token triggers our investigator's special ability, which is usually positive. Skull, Cultist, Tablet, and Elder Thing tokens have scenario-specific negative effects listed on the scenario reference card. The auto-fail token means we fail the test regardless of our skill value.

walkthrough.title: Engagement and Attacks of Opportunity
walkthrough.text: Let's discuss a critical rule about engaged enemies. When an enemy is engaged with us, it is right in our face. If we take any action other than fight, evade, or certain card abilities while engaged, that enemy immediately makes an attack of opportunity against us, dealing its damage and horror values. This punishes us for ignoring threats.

walkthrough.title: Enemies - Hunter Keyword and Spawning
walkthrough.text: Let's learn how enemies behave. When an enemy spawns from the encounter deck, it appears at our location and immediately engages us. Enemies with the Hunter keyword move one location toward the nearest investigator during the Enemy phase. Hunter enemies will chase us across the map if we try to run, so we usually need to deal with them eventually.

walkthrough.title: Enemy Phase
walkthrough.text: First, all Hunter enemies that are not engaged with an investigator move one location toward the nearest investigator. Then, each ready enemy that is engaged with an investigator attacks, dealing its listed damage and horror. This is why leaving enemies engaged with us at the end of our turn is dangerous.

walkthrough.title: Damage and Horror
walkthrough.text: Let's understand how our investigators get hurt. Damage is dealt to our health track, and horror is dealt to our sanity track. If an investigator's damage equals their health, or their horror equals their sanity, that investigator is defeated and eliminated from the scenario. Allies we have in play can absorb some damage and horror for us.

walkthrough.title: Upkeep Phase
walkthrough.text: Each investigator readies all their exhausted cards, turning them upright so they can be used again next round. Then each investigator draws one card and gains one resource for free. This built-in income keeps us moving forward even in the toughest scenarios.

walkthrough.title: The Doom Clock - Agenda Advances
walkthrough.text: When the total doom in play hits the agenda threshold, the agenda flips and something terrible happens. The doom clock creates urgency, and we should never waste actions.

walkthrough.title: The Act Deck - Our Path to Victory
walkthrough.text: Let's understand how we actually win. The act deck shows the clue threshold we need to advance. When we have collectively gathered enough clues, we can spend them to advance the act, which moves the story forward and often opens new locations. Completing all acts in the act deck means we have won the scenario.

walkthrough.title: Winning and Losing
walkthrough.text: Let's be clear about our win and loss conditions. We win by completing the final act in the act deck. We lose if the agenda deck advances past its final card, or if all investigators are defeated. Some scenarios have additional conditions, but these are the core outcomes. The tension between advancing acts and the doom clock is what makes every decision matter.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Play Basics
walkthrough.text: Let's briefly cover what happens between scenarios. After completing a scenario, we earn experience points based on our performance. We spend those experience points to upgrade cards in our decks, swapping in more powerful versions from the collection. Any physical trauma or mental trauma we suffered carries into the next scenario as permanent wounds.

Cycle repeats until scenario ends

walkthrough.title: Welcome to The Gathering
walkthrough.text: We are playing "The Gathering" with two investigators: Roland Banks, the Fed, and Wendy Adams, the Urchin. Roland is strong in combat and intellect, making him our primary fighter and clue gatherer. Wendy is agile and resourceful, with an ability that lets her redraw chaos tokens by discarding cards.

walkthrough.title: The Scenario Begins
walkthrough.text: Let's look at what is in front of us. The agenda deck is set with its doom threshold, the act deck shows how many clues we need to advance, and we are both at the Study location. Our starting location has clues on it already. The encounter deck is shuffled and waiting to ruin our evening.

walkthrough.title: Round One - No Mythos Phase
walkthrough.text: That means no doom is added and no encounter cards are drawn yet. This gives us a precious head start to play assets, gather resources, and position ourselves before the horrors begin. We should use this gift wisely.

walkthrough.title: Roland's First Turn - Setting Up
walkthrough.text: Let's have Roland take his three actions. He spends his first action to play a weapon asset from his hand, paying its resource cost. His second action is an investigate action at the Study, testing his intellect of four against the location's shroud. He draws a minus one token from the chaos bag, still succeeding, and picks up a clue.

walkthrough.title: Roland's Third Action - A Smart Choice
walkthrough.text: He already has a weapon in play and a clue on his card, so building up resources now will let him play more cards next round. This is a balanced opening turn: equip, investigate, and prepare for the future.

walkthrough.title: Wendy's First Turn - Moving and Investigating
walkthrough.text: Let's have Wendy take her three actions. She spends her first action to play an asset, paying its cost. Her second action is to move to an adjacent location, revealing it and placing clues on it. Her third action is to investigate at the new location, using her intellect plus any committed skill cards to try to beat the shroud value.

walkthrough.title: Enemy and Upkeep Phases
walkthrough.text: Let's resolve the Enemy phase. Since no enemies are in play yet, nothing happens. Now we move to Upkeep. Both Roland and Wendy ready all their exhausted cards, draw one card each from their investigator decks, and gain one resource each. This free card and resource each round is our lifeline.

walkthrough.title: Round Two - The Mythos Phase Hits
walkthrough.text: We place one doom token on the agenda card. Then each investigator draws one encounter card. Roland draws a treachery that tests his willpower. Wendy draws an enemy that spawns at her location and immediately engages her. The scenario is now pushing back hard against us.

walkthrough.title: Mistake - Ignoring the Doom Clock
walkthrough.text: Let's say Wendy decides to ignore the enemy engaged with her and instead spends all three actions playing assets and gaining resources, figuring she will deal with it later. Meanwhile, Roland takes his time drawing cards and setting up his board. Neither of us is collecting clues or advancing the act, and doom keeps ticking up each round.

walkthrough.title: Lesson - The Doom Clock Waits for No One
walkthrough.text: That was a mistake. Every round we spend setting up instead of investigating is another doom on the agenda. The doom clock is relentless. We need to balance preparation with progress, because if the agenda advances before we advance the act, the scenario spirals out of control. Always be collecting clues.

walkthrough.title: Wendy's Dangerous Move While Engaged
walkthrough.text: Let's say Wendy, still engaged with that enemy, decides to investigate the location to grab a clue. She announces the investigate action, but wait. She is engaged with an enemy and she just took a non-fight, non-evade action. The enemy immediately makes an attack of opportunity, dealing its damage and horror to Wendy before her investigation even resolves.

walkthrough.title: Lesson - Deal With Engaged Enemies First
walkthrough.text: That hurt. When we are engaged with an enemy, we must fight it, evade it, or accept the attack of opportunity. Taking any other action while engaged is like turning our back on a monster. Wendy should have evaded the enemy first using her strong agility, then investigated safely on her next action.

walkthrough.title: Wendy Evades Correctly This Time
walkthrough.text: Let's rewind and do it right. Wendy spends her first action to evade the enemy, testing her agility of four against its evade value. She draws a zero token from the chaos bag, succeeding easily. The enemy is exhausted and disengaged. Now Wendy can safely spend her remaining two actions investigating without any attacks of opportunity.

walkthrough.title: Roland Fights the Enemy
walkthrough.text: He spends one action to move, then uses his second action to fight, testing his combat value plus his weapon's bonus against the enemy's fight value. He succeeds and deals enough damage to defeat the enemy. His third action goes to investigating the location for another clue.

walkthrough.title: Teamwork in Action
walkthrough.text: Let's appreciate what just happened. Wendy evaded the enemy so she could investigate safely, and Roland came over to finish it off with his superior combat skills. This is cooperative play at its best. Each investigator has strengths and weaknesses, and covering for each other is how we survive.

walkthrough.title: A Tough Skill Test Approaches
walkthrough.text: Let's say Roland attempts an important investigate action at a high-shroud location. His intellect is four and the shroud is three, so he only needs to draw a zero or better to succeed. He could commit skill cards from his hand to boost his value, making it safer. But he decides to save his cards for later, figuring the test is easy enough.

walkthrough.title: Mistake - Over-Committing and Under-Committing
walkthrough.text: Let's say Roland draws the minus three token and fails. That clue stays on the location and he wasted an action. On the very next test, a critical fight against a dangerous enemy, Roland panics and commits every skill card in his hand to guarantee success. He defeats the enemy, but now his hand is empty and he has nothing left for the treachery tests coming in the next Mythos phase.

walkthrough.title: Lesson - Budget Your Skill Cards
walkthrough.text: That was a two-part mistake. First, Roland should have committed at least one card to the investigate test to protect against bad token draws. Second, he should not have dumped his entire hand into one fight. We need to spread our skill cards across multiple tests, always keeping at least one or two in reserve for unexpected challenges.

walkthrough.title: Advancing the Act
walkthrough.text: Let's collect the final clues we need. Between Roland's investigations and Wendy's clue gathering, we have met the threshold on the act card. We spend the required clues and advance the act, flipping it to reveal the next stage of the story. New locations may open up, and the narrative moves forward. This is our reward for efficient play.

walkthrough.title: Checking the Doom Clock
walkthrough.text: Let's count the doom on the agenda. Several rounds have passed and doom has been accumulating. We are two doom away from the agenda advancing, which means we have roughly two more rounds before something terrible happens. This pressure is what makes every single action decision so meaningful in this game.

walkthrough.title: The Final Push
walkthrough.text: Let's race to finish. Roland and Wendy split up to cover more ground, each investigating different locations to gather the remaining clues. Wendy uses her ability to redraw a bad chaos token, turning a failed test into a success. Roland uses his last weapon charge to defeat an enemy blocking his path. We spend our final clues to advance the last act.

walkthrough.title: Scenario Complete
walkthrough.text: Let's celebrate, we completed The Gathering. We read the resolution text based on our outcome and record any experience points earned. We also note any trauma suffered by our investigators. Roland took some physical trauma and Wendy took some mental trauma, but both survived. These injuries will follow us into the next scenario of the campaign.

walkthrough.title: What We Learned
walkthrough.text: Let's recap our three key lessons. First, never ignore the doom clock because wasting actions on setup while doom ticks will cost us the scenario. Second, always deal with engaged enemies before taking other actions, or we suffer attacks of opportunity. Third, budget our skill cards across multiple tests instead of going all-in on one and having nothing left for the next.

walkthrough.title: Before Your Next Investigation
walkthrough.text:
Before we dive into the next scenario, here are three things that will help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy fundamentals and the most common pitfalls to avoid.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to phases, actions, chaos tokens, and card types.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Now go save Arkham.

walkthrough.title: Respect the Doom Clock
walkthrough.text: The single most important lesson in Arkham Horror is that the doom clock never stops. One doom lands on the agenda every round no matter what, and some encounter cards add even more. Every action we spend drawing cards, gaining resources, or setting up our board is an action we did not spend collecting clues. We must always be making progress toward the act deck, even if our board is not fully set up yet.

walkthrough.title: Always Deal With Engaged Enemies
walkthrough.text: Never try to take a non-combat action while an enemy is engaged with us. The attack of opportunity will deal damage and horror before our action even resolves, and we still have to complete the action afterward. If we need to investigate or move, we must either fight the enemy to defeat it, evade it to disengage, or accept the punishment. Wendy should evade with her high agility, and Roland should fight with his combat strength.

walkthrough.title: Budget Skill Cards Across Tests
walkthrough.text: Committing cards to skill tests is how we control the randomness of the chaos bag, but every committed card is gone after the test. We should commit one or two cards to important tests as insurance, not zero and not our whole hand. A good rule of thumb is to aim for a skill value two or three higher than the difficulty, which lets us absorb most negative tokens while keeping cards in reserve for the next challenge.

walkthrough.title: Play to Each Investigator's Strengths
walkthrough.text: Roland has high combat and solid intellect, so he should be fighting enemies and investigating medium-shroud locations. Wendy has high agility and a chaos token redraw ability, so she should be evading enemies and handling risky tests where the redraw might save us. When we lean into each investigator's strengths, we get more value out of every action than if we try to make everyone do everything.

walkthrough.title: Get Key Assets Into Play Early
walkthrough.text: Weapons, investigation tools, and allies are the assets that define our effectiveness for the entire scenario. Getting a weapon on Roland in round one means he can fight enemies efficiently for the rest of the game. Getting an ally into play means we have a damage or horror soak for emergencies. We should prioritize playing our most impactful assets in the first two rounds, even if it means spending an action or two on resources to afford them.

walkthrough.title: Clues Are the Priority
walkthrough.text: Defeating enemies keeps us alive, but collecting clues is what actually wins the game. Every clue we pick up is progress toward advancing the act. If we have a choice between defeating a non-threatening enemy and investigating for a clue, the clue is almost always the right call. Enemies that are not Hunter can be left behind when we move to a new location, while clues are only available where we are standing right now.

walkthrough.title: Use Allies as Damage and Horror Saks
walkthrough.text: Allies in play can absorb damage and horror that would otherwise land on our investigator. This is incredibly valuable because an investigator defeated by damage or horror is out of the game. When we have an ally with two health and two sanity, that is essentially four extra hits we can survive. We should always consider assigning damage or horror to allies before our investigator, especially when our health or sanity is getting low.

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Here are the traps that catch new investigators. First, spending too many early actions on drawing cards and gaining resources while doom ticks up. Second, taking investigate or move actions while engaged with an enemy and eating an attack of opportunity. Third, committing zero cards to a test and then over-committing everything to the next one. Fourth, spreading investigators to opposite corners of the map where they cannot help each other. Fifth, forgetting that upkeep gives a free card and resource, so we do not need to spend as many actions on economy as we think.

summary.title: Respect the Doom Clock
bullets: 1 doom per round, minimum — clock never stops
Every action not spent on clues = falling behind
Always be making progress toward the act deck

summary.title: Always Deal With Engaged Enemies
bullets: Non-combat actions while engaged → attack of opportunity
Fight (Roland) or evade (Wendy) before doing anything else
Accepting the attack is almost never worth it

summary.title: Budget Skill Cards
bullets: Commit 1-2 cards to important tests as insurance
Aim for skill value 2-3 above difficulty to absorb bad tokens
Never dump entire hand into one test — keep reserves

summary.title: Play to Investigator Strengths
bullets: Roland: fight enemies + investigate medium-shroud locations
Wendy: evade enemies + handle risky tests (redraw ability)
Lean into strengths for maximum action efficiency

summary.title: Get Key Assets Out Early
bullets: Weapons, investigation tools, allies = top priority rounds 1-2
Spend actions on resources early to afford key cards
A weapon on Roland round 1 pays off all game

summary.title: Clues Are the Priority
bullets: Clues advance the act deck = how we win
Non-Hunter enemies can be left behind when moving
Choose clue-gathering over non-essential combat

summary.title: Use Allies as Soak
bullets: Allies absorb damage/horror that would hit investigator
Ally with 2 health + 2 sanity = 4 extra hits of survival
Assign damage/horror to allies when health/sanity is low

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Too much setup while doom ticks — investigate early
Actions while engaged → attack of opportunity
All-or-nothing skill card commits — spread them out
Splitting too far apart — stay close enough to help each other
Forgetting upkeep gives free card + resource each round

walkthrough.title: Action Economy and Compression
walkthrough.text: We only get three actions per round, plus whatever the upkeep phase gives us for free. Cards and abilities that give us extra actions or combine multiple effects into one action are disproportionately powerful. A card that lets us investigate and move in the same action effectively gives us four actions worth of value in a three-action turn. We should prioritize cards that compress multiple effects into single actions.

walkthrough.title: Doom Management and Strategic Risk
walkthrough.text: Let's go deeper on doom. Some player cards and abilities add doom as a cost. This is dangerous but can be worthwhile if the benefit saves us more actions than the doom costs in time. A card that adds one doom but saves us three actions worth of work is a net positive. The key is tracking exactly how many rounds we have left before the agenda advances and whether we can afford to shorten that window. Never add doom if we are within one round of the threshold.

walkthrough.title: Skill Test Math and Token Probability
walkthrough.text: Let's understand the math behind the chaos bag. On standard difficulty, most negative tokens range from minus one to minus four, with the auto-fail and special symbols adding worse outcomes. If our skill value is two above the difficulty, we pass on everything except the worst tokens. If we are three or four above, we pass on nearly everything except auto-fail. We should commit cards to reach the two-above threshold on important tests and the four-above threshold on critical ones.

walkthrough.title: Investigator Pairing and Role Division
walkthrough.text: Let's optimize our team composition. The strongest investigator pairings have one character focused on clue-gathering and another on enemy management. Roland Banks as the fighter and Daisy Walker as the dedicated researcher is a classic pairing. Wendy Adams is a flex investigator who can evade enemies and grab clues in a pinch. We should assign roles clearly: one investigator handles enemies so the other can investigate without interruption.

walkthrough.title: Deck Construction for Campaign Success
walkthrough.text: Let's plan our decks for the entire campaign, not just one scenario. In early scenarios, we need cheap, efficient cards that help us survive and gather clues. Our experience points should go toward upgrading key cards that we play in every game. A level-two weapon that costs one less resource and deals one more damage will save us dozens of actions over a campaign. We should plan an upgrade path before the first scenario and adjust based on what challenges we face.

walkthrough.title: When to Evade Instead of Fight
walkthrough.text: Let's master evasion timing. Fighting costs actions and cards to deal enough damage, and some enemies have high health pools that make them expensive to defeat. Evading costs one action and one test, and it buys us a full round of safety since the enemy stays exhausted through the Enemy phase. We should evade when the enemy has high health but low evade value, when we need to move past an enemy without spending multiple fight actions, or when we are low on health and cannot risk a failed fight test.

walkthrough.title: Location Movement Efficiency
walkthrough.text: Let's optimize our movement across the scenario map. Every move action we spend is an action we did not spend investigating or fighting. We should plan our routes to minimize backtracking, clear all clues from a location before moving on, and avoid splitting up unless both investigators can handle enemies independently. In scenarios with many locations, the investigator with higher agility should scout ahead while the other follows once the path is clear.

walkthrough.title: Managing Horror and Sanity Loss
walkthrough.text: Let's address the often-overlooked sanity track. New players focus on health but forget that horror defeats investigators just as permanently. Many treacheries from the encounter deck deal horror rather than damage, and some enemies deal more horror than damage on their attacks. We should include sanity soak allies in our decks, commit to willpower tests against horror treacheries seriously, and keep track of both health and sanity margins throughout the game. Running low on sanity with two agenda stages left is a losing position.

walkthrough.title: Reading the Encounter Deck
walkthrough.text: Each scenario lists which encounter sets are included, and each set has a theme. If we know the Rats set is in the deck, we expect low-health enemies that clog our board. If we know the Ancient Evils set is included, we must expect cards that add extra doom. We should read the encounter set contents before the scenario begins and plan our decks to counter the most dangerous cards we might draw.

walkthrough.title: Campaign Trauma Management
walkthrough.text: Let's plan for the long game. Physical and mental trauma carry between scenarios, permanently reducing our starting health and sanity. An investigator who takes two physical trauma starts every future scenario with two fewer health. This means we must weigh short-term scenario success against long-term campaign viability. Sometimes it is better to take a worse scenario resolution that preserves our investigators' health than to push for the best outcome and accumulate crippling trauma that dooms us in later scenarios.

summary.title: Action Economy and Compression
bullets: 3 actions per round + upkeep freebies = tight budget
Cards that combine effects (investigate + move) = massive value
Prioritize action-compressing cards in deck construction

summary.title: Doom Management
bullets: Some cards add doom as cost — acceptable if benefit > time lost
Track exact rounds until agenda advances
Never add voluntary doom within 1 round of threshold

summary.title: Skill Test Probability
bullets: Skill +2 above difficulty → pass on most tokens
Skill +4 above → pass on nearly everything except auto-fail
+2 for important tests, +4 for critical tests

summary.title: Investigator Pairing
bullets: Best pairings: dedicated clue-gatherer + dedicated fighter
Assign roles clearly so one investigates uninterrupted
Flex investigators (Wendy) fill gaps in either role

summary.title: Deck Construction for Campaign
bullets: Plan upgrade path before scenario 1
Early: cheap efficient cards; later: powerful XP upgrades
Upgraded weapons/tools save dozens of actions over a campaign

summary.title: Evade vs. Fight
bullets: Evade when: high HP enemy, low evade value, need to move past, low on health
Evaded enemies stay exhausted through Enemy phase = 1 round safety
Fighting is better when enemy must be removed permanently

summary.title: Movement Efficiency
bullets: Every move = 1 lost investigate/fight action
Clear all clues from location before moving on
Minimize backtracking — plan routes through the map

summary.title: Horror and Sanity Management
bullets: Horror defeats investigators just like damage
Include sanity soak allies in deck
Commit seriously to willpower tests vs. horror treacheries

summary.title: Reading the Encounter Deck
bullets: Review encounter sets before scenario starts
Ancient Evils = expect extra doom; Rats = expect swarm enemies
Build deck to counter the most dangerous encounter cards

summary.title: Campaign Trauma Management
bullets: Trauma persists — 2 physical trauma = 2 less starting health forever
Sometimes accept a worse resolution to preserve investigator viability
Long-term campaign health > short-term scenario perfection

entries.definition: The first phase of each round (skipped on round one). Place one doom on the agenda, check if total doom meets the agenda threshold, then each investigator draws one encounter card. This is the game's primary way of escalating pressure.

The second phase of each round. Each investigator takes three actions in player order. Actions can be any combination of draw, resource, play, move, investigate, fight, evade, engage, or activate. This is the players' main opportunity to make progress.

The third phase of each round. First, all Hunter enemies not engaged with an investigator move one location toward the nearest investigator. Then, all ready enemies engaged with investigators attack, dealing their printed damage and horror values.

The fourth and final phase of each round. Each investigator readies all their exhausted cards, draws one card from their investigator deck, and gains one resource token. This free income keeps investigators functional even in tough scenarios.

Spend one action to draw one card from your investigator deck. Cards are your tools, weapons, and skill commits, so maintaining hand size is important. The upkeep phase also gives a free draw each round.
Spend one action to gain one resource token from the token pool. Resources pay for playing cards from your hand. The upkeep phase also gives one free resource each round.

Spend one action and pay the card's resource cost to play an asset or event from your hand. Assets enter play and stay until discarded or defeated. Events resolve their text and go to the discard pile.
Spend one action to perform an intellect skill test against the location's shroud value. On success, take one clue from the location. Clues are spent to advance the act deck and win the scenario.

Spend one action to move your investigator to a connected location. If the new location is unrevealed, flip it face up and place clue tokens on it. Investigators can only interact with cards at their current location.

Spend one action to perform a combat skill test against an engaged enemy's fight value. On success, deal one damage to the enemy plus any bonus damage from weapons. An enemy is defeated when its damage equals its health.

Spend one action to perform an agility skill test against an engaged enemy's evade value. On success, the enemy is exhausted and disengaged. It will not attack during the Enemy phase this round. It readies during the next upkeep.

Spend one action to engage an enemy at your location, pulling it off another investigator and onto yourself. Useful for protecting a fragile investigator or setting up a fight with a weapon-equipped character.

Spend one action to trigger an ability on a card in play that has an action arrow printed on it. Many assets have powerful activate abilities that require an action to use, such as using charges on a spell or tool.

The sequence for resolving any skill test: (1) determine the investigator's base skill value and the test difficulty, (2) commit cards with matching skill icons from hand, (3) draw one chaos token from the bag, (4) apply the token's modifier, (5) compare total to difficulty. Success if total is greater than or equal to difficulty.

Before drawing the chaos token, the investigator may commit any number of cards from hand that have matching skill icons in their upper left corner. Each icon adds one to the investigator's skill value for that test. Committed cards are always discarded after the test, win or lose.

Icons printed in the upper left of player cards representing the four main skills: willpower (book), intellect (magnifying glass), combat (fist), and agility (foot). Wild icons count as any skill. These icons are used when committing cards to skill tests.

Chaos tokens printed with negative numbers (typically -1 through -8, depending on difficulty). When drawn during a skill test, the number is added to the investigator's skill value as a modifier. Higher difficulty settings include more severe negative numbers.

A chaos token that triggers the investigator's unique Elder Sign ability printed on their investigator card. Usually a positive effect, such as a bonus to the skill value or an additional benefit on success. Each investigator has a different Elder Sign effect.

A chaos token that causes the skill test to automatically fail regardless of the investigator's skill value. There is exactly one auto-fail token in every chaos bag at every difficulty level. It cannot be mitigated by high skill values or committed cards.

A scenario-specific chaos token with a negative effect defined on the scenario reference card. The skull's modifier changes from scenario to scenario and often scales with game state, such as the number of enemies in play.
A scenario-specific chaos token with a negative effect defined on the scenario reference card. Like the skull, its effect varies by scenario and difficulty level.
A scenario-specific chaos token with a negative effect defined on the scenario reference card. Typically appears in scenarios with more complex chaos bag configurations.
A scenario-specific chaos token with a negative effect defined on the scenario reference card. Usually the most punishing of the symbol tokens, often appearing on harder difficulty levels.

A player card type that stays in play after being played. Assets include weapons, tools, allies, and other items. They occupy slots (hand, arcane, accessory, ally, body) and provide ongoing abilities, skill boosts, or soak for damage and horror.

A player card type that resolves its effect immediately when played and then goes to the discard pile. Events provide one-time benefits such as bonus clues, enemy damage, movement, or resource generation.

A player card type that can only be committed to skill tests, not played as an action. Skill cards typically have multiple skill icons and a bonus effect that triggers if the test succeeds. They are discarded after the test resolves.

An encounter card type that takes effect immediately when drawn during the Mythos phase. Treacheries may test a skill (often willpower), deal damage or horror, add doom, or impose negative conditions. They are discarded or attached to a card depending on their text.

An encounter card type that spawns as an enemy in play when drawn during the Mythos phase. Enemy cards show fight value, evade value, health, and the damage and horror dealt per attack. Enemies engage investigators at their location.

A double-sided card representing a place on the scenario map. The unrevealed side shows the location name. The revealed side shows shroud value (difficulty to investigate), clue value (clues placed when revealed), and any special abilities.

An enemy keyword. During the Enemy phase, a Hunter enemy that is not engaged with an investigator moves one location toward the nearest investigator. Hunter enemies pursue investigators across the map and cannot be permanently avoided by simply moving away.

An enemy keyword. When an investigator fails a fight test against a Retaliate enemy, that enemy immediately deals its attack (damage and horror) to the investigator. Makes fighting risky for investigators with low combat skill.

An enemy keyword. When an investigator fails an evade test against an Alert enemy, that enemy immediately deals its attack (damage and horror) to the investigator. Makes evasion risky against this enemy type.

An enemy keyword. A Massive enemy is considered engaged with all investigators at its location simultaneously. It does not exhaust when evaded by a single investigator and attacks everyone during the Enemy phase.

An enemy keyword. An Aloof enemy does not automatically engage investigators at its location. Investigators must use the engage action to engage it before they can fight it. Aloof enemies often block locations without directly threatening investigators.

Earned after completing a scenario, based on the victory points of defeated enemies and completed objectives. XP is spent between scenarios to upgrade cards in the investigator's deck. Higher-level cards cost more XP but are significantly more powerful.

Between scenarios, spend XP to swap cards in your deck for higher-level versions. The XP cost equals the difference in level between the new card and the card it replaces. Level-zero cards can be swapped freely. Building a strong upgrade path is key to campaign success.

A permanent injury suffered when an investigator is defeated by damage during a scenario. Each point of physical trauma reduces the investigator's starting health in all future scenarios by one. Accumulating too much trauma can make an investigator unplayable.

A permanent injury suffered when an investigator is defeated by horror during a scenario. Each point of mental trauma reduces the investigator's starting sanity in all future scenarios by one. Like physical trauma, too much mental trauma can end an investigator's career.

The narrative outcome read at the end of a scenario, determined by how the investigators performed. Different resolutions lead to different story paths, rewards, and consequences in the campaign. There are usually multiple possible resolutions per scenario.

Tokens placed on the agenda card (one per round during Mythos) and sometimes on enemies or other cards. When total doom in play meets the agenda threshold, the agenda advances. Doom represents the encroaching darkness and creates the game's core time pressure.

Tokens placed on revealed locations and collected by investigators through successful investigate actions. Clues are spent to advance the act deck. Collecting clues efficiently is the primary path to winning a scenario.

A deck of cards representing the scenario's evil plan. One doom is placed on it each round. When total doom meets the threshold, the agenda advances, usually triggering a negative event. If the agenda advances past the final card, the investigators lose.

A deck of cards representing the investigators' progress through the scenario. Advancing acts requires spending collected clues. Each act advancement moves the story forward and may reveal new locations. Completing the final act wins the scenario.

A number printed on a revealed location card representing how difficult it is to investigate there. When performing an investigate action, the investigator's intellect is tested against the location's shroud value. Higher shroud means harder investigation.

The state of an enemy being directly confronting an investigator. Engaged enemies attack during the Enemy phase and trigger attacks of opportunity when the investigator takes non-fight, non-evade actions. Enemies engage when spawned or when an investigator enters their location.

A free attack made by an engaged enemy whenever the engaged investigator takes any action other than fight, evade, or certain card abilities. The enemy deals its full damage and horror values. Avoiding attacks of opportunity is critical to survival.

Cards in play are either ready (upright) or exhausted (turned sideways). Exhausted cards cannot use their abilities or attack. Cards are exhausted when used and readied during the Upkeep phase. Evaded enemies become exhausted.

A bag or container holding the chaos tokens used for skill tests. Its contents are determined by the scenario and difficulty level. One token is drawn randomly for each skill test. The chaos bag is the game's primary source of randomness and uncertainty.

Asset cards occupy equipment slots on an investigator: two hand slots, one ally slot, one accessory slot, one body slot, and two arcane slots. An investigator cannot have more assets than their slots allow. Exceeding a slot requires discarding an existing asset in that slot.
